content.full_text:
FATn PoLITICAL PnaCTICES CoTvTTT,TISSIoN
428 J Street. Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
August 20,2003
Councilmember Doug Tessitor
P. O. Box 938
Glendora, CA 91740
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-03-167
Dear Mr. Tessitor:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTIONS
1. May you participate in decisions relating to Colley Ford?
2. Would your familial relationship with the owners of Colley Ford be sufficient
to create the perception of a conflict of interest in decisions regarding Colley
Ford?
3. Do contributions to your election campaign committee from the owners of
Colley Ford constitute gifts to you as an individual?
4. Do contributions to your election campaign committee from the owners of
Colley Ford require you to abstain from participating in decisions regarding
Colley Ford?
CONCLUSION
Because neither the investment interests of your brothers-in-law nor campaign
contributions received from your brothers-in-law are economic interests under the Act,
' Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appeu at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
,@


File No. A-03-167
Page No. 2
they will not prgvent you from participating in decisions regarding the proposed tax
participation agreement between the City of Glendora and the Glendora Auto Center.
FACTS
You are a council member for the City of Glendora. As a council member, you
will soon be asked to consider entering into a tax participation agreernent between the
City of Glendora and the Glendora Auto Center. Your brothers-inJaw own 100 percent
of Colley Auto Cars, Inc. (d.b.a. Colley ForO. Colley Ford is a partner in the Glendora
Auto center and a principal in the potential agreement with the city.
No member of your immediate family (your wife or children) has any financial
investment or interest in this business in any form. Neither you nor your wife have
received any income, loans or gifts from the business or from your brothers-in-law. your
election campaign, the Committee to Elect Doug Tessitor, received contributions of
$ 1,000 each from Carl Colley and Fred Colley personally (as opposed to through the
corporation) in February 2003. We assume, although it is not stated in your letter, that
Carl and Fred Colley are your brothers-in-law.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making, participating.in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
d-ecision in"which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted a
standard, eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualiSring
conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision. (Regulation 1g700(bxl)-(g).)
1 and 2. Are you a oopublic official" and are you making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?
These first two steps are met under your facts. As a council member for the City
of Glendora, you are a "public official." (Sections 82048,8204L) By deliberating and
voting on decisions regarding the proposed tax participation agreement, you would be
engaging in conduct subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(I).)
3. Economic interests
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. A public official has an economic interest in:


a
File No. A-03-167
Page No. 3
A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect2 investment of $2,000
or more (section 87r03(a); regulation 18703..1(a)); or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
(section 87 1 03(d); regulation 1 8703. 1 (b));
Real property in which he or she has a direct or indiiect interest of $2,000 or more
(section 871 03(b); regulation 157 03.2);
Any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c); regulation
18703.3);
a
a
a
o
Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12
months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);
His or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this
is the "personal financial effects"3 rule (section87l}3;regulation 18703.5i.
We have no facts to suggest that any of these enumerated economic interests are
implicated in decisions regarding the proposed tax participation agreement.
Would yourfamilial relationship with the owners of Coltey Ford be sfficient to
credte the perception of a conflict of interest in decisions regarding Colley Ford?
Although your city may choose on its own to require additional safeguards to
avoid ethical conflicts of interest, including the appearance of bias, the Act only prohibits
financial conflicts of interest, not personal inclinations toward one side or the other of an
issue. As defined by the Act, a public official's interests do not extend to the interests of
the official's in-laws.
Do contributions to your election campaign committeefrom the owners of Colley
Ford constitute gifts to you as an individual?
Campaign contributions are specifically exempted from the definitions of the
terms "income" and "gift" in sections 82030(bX1) and82028(bX3), respectively. (See
Woodland Hills Residents Assn. v. City Council of Los Angeles (1980) 26 Ca1.3d938.)
2 For purposes of section 87lO3,"indirect investment or interest means any investrnent or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child ofa public official, by an agent on behalfofa public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater.,,
3 In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103,a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict ofinterest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-month period.


File No. A-03-167
Page No. 4
Because a campaign contribution is not an economic interest, it does not create a conflict
of interest for you under section 87100.
The only instance in which campaign contributions may cause a conflict of
interest for an elected official under the Act is if the elected official has also been
appointed to sit on a board or commission that makes decisions concerning licenses,
permits, and other entitlements for use. (Section 84308.) For example, a city council
member or a mayor may be appointed to sit on a planning commission, a cable
commission, or a local area formation commission and, in this circumstance, may be
disqualified from parlicrpatingin a decision before the commission if he or she has
received a contribution of more than $250 from a party or participant to the proceeding.
Enclosed is a fact sheet from the Commission's website regarding disqualification under
section 84308. In addition, some cities and counties have enacted local ordinances that
require disqualification regarding sources of campaign contributions in instances that
would not be required by the Act.
We have not gone onto analyze the remaining steps in the eight-step analysis
since your question does not relate to an "economic interest" as defined in the Act.
Enclosed is the Commission fact sheet entitled "Can I Vote? Overview of the Conflicts
Laws," which details all eight steps for future reference.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
cacta e
Qho"qn n
By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant4
Enclosures
AKje

CONCLUSION: Because neither the investment interests of your brothers-in-law nor campaign
